item.title: Police should swiftly arrest those making threats against Fiji's peace - Turaga
item.description: Rashika Kumar , Antornette Vuniuca · 20/07/2026 Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga says if there are people making inflammatory comments against the peace of this country, then the police should be swift and take the person into police custody.
item.content:encoded:
Rashika Kumar , Antornette Vuniuca · 20/07/2026

Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga says if there are people making inflammatory comments against the peace of this country, then the police should be swift and take the person into police custody.

This comes as the police have arrested a man for allegedly issuing a threat on social media to remove Fiji's Constitution or he will burn down the nation's capital.

Turaga says nobody is supposed to be making those threats and the government is steadfast.

He says there are no issues with the constitutional review process, which remains on schedule for a 31st August submission to the President.

Turaga stresses that if people have any concerns, he assures them that this government came with the promise to deliver a new Constitution, and they are doing that.

He also stresses that any unnecessary threats are irresponsible.

The Minister for Justice is urging people of Fiji to refrain from making inflammatory comments or threats that undermine peace and security.

He adds that we have enjoyed a wonderful environment of collaboration between the government and the security forces, and will continue to maintain that.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Alternative LANA arrangements confirmed for affected Taveuni students
item.description: Students in Years 5 and 7 who are unable to attend the Literacy and Numeracy Assessment (LANA) on 21st and 22nd July due to verified transport disruptions beyond their control will be considered under the Ministry of Education's Assessment Policy. The Ministry of Education says alternative arrangements will be made for eligible students to sit the assessment on a later approved date, ensuring they
item.content:encoded:
Students in Years 5 and 7 who are unable to attend the Literacy and Numeracy Assessment (LANA) on 21st and 22nd July due to verified transport disruptions beyond their control will be considered under the Ministry of Education's Assessment Policy.

The Ministry of Education says alternative arrangements will be made for eligible students to sit the assessment on a later approved date, ensuring they are not unfairly disadvantaged.

They are working closely with the Office of the Commissioner Northern, the Land Transport Authority (LTA), the Fiji Police Force, school leaders, and other key stakeholders to minimise the impact on students and ensure continuity of learning.

The Ministry is coordinating a range of contingency measures, including transport assistance where required, close monitoring of student attendance and transport services, and ongoing collaboration with schools and partner agencies to support affected communities.

Permanent Secretary for Education, Navin Raj, says the Ministry's priority is to ensure every student has safe and uninterrupted access to education and that no child is disadvantaged by circumstances beyond their control.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Kamikamica trial to begin tomorrow after court allows amended charges
item.description: The trial for former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica will begin properly tomorrow after Suva Magistrate Charles Ratakele allowed FICAC to file amended charges today. Magistrate Ratakele had earlier adjourned for 45 minutes while he made his decision regarding the amended charges.
item.content:encoded:
The trial for former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica will begin properly tomorrow after Suva Magistrate Charles Ratakele allowed FICAC to file amended charges today.

Magistrate Ratakele had earlier adjourned for 45 minutes while he made his decision regarding the amended charges.

FICAC counsel Josann Pene had asked for 14 days to serve the defence with the amended charges but defence counsel Wylie Clarke said they are still ready to go to trial.

Clarke says the sooner this is done the better, while all the information is still fresh.

Earlier, Pene had asked if they can amend the charges due to new information that they have just received this morning.

Clarke objected to this saying that they have spent their time and the whole weekend preparing for this trial on the agreed documents agreed to from last week.

Kamikamica is charged with perjury - Contrary to section 176(1) of the Crimes Act no. 44 of 2009.

According to the amended charge, Kamikamica between 1st December 2024 to 31st March 2025 at Suva in the Central Division, being lawfully sworn as a witness in a judicial proceeding namely a Commission of Inquiry into the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner, wilfully made a material statement on oath, namely, that he had no knowledge of the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner prior to her appointment dated 5th September 2024.

He is also charged with giving false information to a public servant where it is alleged that Kamikamica between 1st December 2024 to 31st March 2025, being lawfully a sworn as a witness in a judicial proceeding namely Commission of Inquiry into the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner, gave to the Commissioner of Inquiry (being a person employed in the civil service) information he knew or believed to be false, namely that he had no knowledge of the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner prior to her appointment dated 5th September 2024.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Peacekeeping must adapt to new security threats - Turaga
item.description: The conference, co-hosted by the Ministry of Defence and Veterans Affairs and the Stimson Center with support from the Australian Department of Defence and Global Affairs Canada, has brought together government representatives, United Nations officials, regional organisations, peacekeeping training centres and researchers to discuss the future of peacekeeping and the protection of civilians. Turag
item.content:encoded:
The conference, co-hosted by the Ministry of Defence and Veterans Affairs and the Stimson Center with support from the Australian Department of Defence and Global Affairs Canada, has brought together government representatives, United Nations officials, regional organisations, peacekeeping training centres and researchers to discuss the future of peacekeeping and the protection of civilians.

Turaga says today's peacekeepers face challenges that did not exist, when Fiji first deployed troops to Lebanon in 1978.

According to Turaga, peacekeepers now contend with misinformation, organised crime, armed drones, climate-related instability and increasingly complex conflicts while operating with tighter budgets and in more dangerous environments.

Despite these challenges, he says civilians continue to look to United Nations peacekeepers for protection, stability and hope.

Turaga says Fiji's contribution to United Nations peacekeeping spans more than five decades, with more than 15,000 Fijians serving in missions across the Middle East, Africa, Asia and other parts of the world.

He says more than 65 Fijians have lost their lives while serving on peacekeeping missions, and their sacrifice continues to inspire Fiji's commitment to global peace and security.

Turaga says Fiji remains committed to the vision of the Pacific as an Ocean of Peace , where dialogue, cooperation and mutual respect are used to resolve differences rather than conflict.

He also revealed that Fiji is developing its first National Peace Operations Strategy, to guide the country's future contributions to international peacekeeping.

Acting President and CEO of the Stimson Center Rachel Stohl says the world is at a critical point for peacekeeping as United Nations missions face funding shortages, growing political divisions and increasing security challenges.

She says while peacekeeping missions have fewer resources, the demand for them has not declined, with conflicts, displacement and humanitarian crises continuing to increase around the world.

Stohl says peacekeeping operations must continue to evolve, so they remain effective and able to respond to future challenges.

The conference continues over the next few days, with delegates discussing the future of peace operations, the protection of civilians and evolving security challenges across the Indo-Pacific region.

item.title: Manoa Kamikamica's alleged perjury trial begins in Suva
item.description: Former Deputy Prime Minister, Manoa Kamikamica's trial starts this morning in the Suva Magistrates Court.He is charged with perjur
